## [00:00 - 03:00]

I am so excited to welcome to the Masterminds Studio, Dr. Tina. Hey, Dr. Tina is the sole doctor, a published author, the creator of the award-winning Soul Detox Method, founder of Soul Detox & Company, and a master coach for the last 18 years. I love that. Oh my gosh, that's incredible. And whether it's working with a nation's first family or coaching a mother starting her first business, Dr. Tina empowers leaders to do the hard work of transforming their spirit, soul and body to create both financial and emotional freedom. Welcome, Dr. Tina. Thank you. I'm so excited to have you here. Thank you. It's a wonderful being here. Thank you for flying in from Michigan to Scottsdale and joining us for this interview. We are going to be talking about your journey as a coach. So tell me a little bit about you and how you started coaching, how that even came about. Well, a little bit about myself is I happened into coaching. It wasn't even something that I pursued, it was something that found me. So I had actually gone to a five-day personal development conference. Just for my own self, I went with my husband. And while we were there at that conference, we were taken out onto a ropes course. And it was like, you can just imagine in your mind going to like a military training camp, like a boot camp. And that's where we were literally brought out into. Yeah, we were brought out into the woods. There were 70 students that were a part of this class that we were taking. They were ranging in age from 18 to 70. And we got put in this one exercise on the ropes course that completely changed my life. I ended up getting offered a job as soon as this exercise was done. And here's what the exercise was. There were 70 of us brought to the wall. It was a 12-foot wall. It didn't have any ropes. It didn't have any protections around. But all we were told by our instructors out on the ropes course is, here's what you have. You have less than five minutes to come up with a strategy of how you're going to get everyone over the wall. You don't pass until you get done. Okay. Everybody has to make it over and will go as long as it takes in order to get everybody there. And, oh, by the way, you have to do it without speaking. Oh my gosh. So in that moment, all of a sudden, I watched this whole entire class freak out on height issues, physical ailments, their weight, people helping them go over such something so huge. And all of a sudden, within me, my own past experience, I was a cheerleader for many years when I was in high school and I did competitive cheerleading. And so I just saw it. I saw exactly what needed to be done. And I jumped into the middle and began giving everybody the instructions. And then they said, no one speak. And then this mastery skill of being able to read people and watching their emotions and looking at their body language and what their body language was communicating to me.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

Me just looking into the eyes of people or telling them to look into the eyes of me and then communicating to people, you can trust me. Come on, we can do this. I'm not going to let you fall. And I had no idea even what that was. But that's what started it for me. And then that's what ended up birthing the coaching opportunity. Did you at that point know what coaching was? Were you familiar with the whole industry of coaching? I wasn't. Wow. I wasn't. I had done a lot of training. I had done a lot of teaching. I had done a lot of that. But as far as working one-on-one, that was my introduction into the life coaching world. And so then at that point, I pursued it. I'm like, if I'm going to do this, I'm going to do this with my everything and found a university that I was connected with and ended up getting certified through them, went through a full round of training. Because if I was going to do it, I wanted to do it right. A lot of people who are coming through Mastermind Business System at this stage in the program are wondering, is coaching really the right thing for me? And what was it about coaching that made you feel like this is my next right step? Well, I have a passion for training and I had a passion for teaching. And so I was already doing that as a career and even worked on the ministry side of things as well. And what I began to notice is that when I would teach on a topic and anybody who teaches has had this experience, when you teach on a topic, there was always the line of people who were there waiting to ask their specific questions. It was usually questions that couldn't be asked publicly or they were had their own issue on the inside of themselves that they didn't want to bring that forth to everybody. They wanted to hold that. And so that one-on-one conversation caused me to just take a step back and go, okay, well, group teaching is amazing. Amazing. However, for personal transformation or for personal application, accountability, or the places where we all get stuck at different times in life, bringing in that 360 feedback, that's what caused me to go, I think we need to create a one-on-one coaching program in addition to the group classes that we are teaching. Right. And so I can see that a lot of times that people who have been in a teaching field that they

## [06:03 - 09:05]

enjoy sharing their knowledge, it's a natural transition over to coaching. Do you think that's true? Have you seen that as well? I have and I haven't. I think that's twofold because there are some people who are amazing trainers. They're amazing teachers. But if you get them into the one-on-one aspect that I believe really, it forces you to go from your head down into your heart. There's some trainers who are very gifted at giving content information, but I believe coaching is about stepping into that deeper level of who someone is, into their personality. Do you know how to read people? Do you know how to get into the heart, into just the guts of who they are? Are you willing to just roll up your sleeves a little bit and get dirty with that person? Become that co-thinker in exactly in that space where they might feel stuck or they have a place of pain for what it is that I do. I don't think every trainer or every teacher can do that. But if you have a heart for people and you want to be able to watch them go into that next level, then that's where I'm like, okay, coaching is a beautiful opportunity for us to step into. Let's go back and talk about your first coaching program that you offered. Did you think I'm putting together a program or did you just find a person who you could coach or talk me through that? The classes that I was teaching, those people who were coming up to me one-on-one and saying, Tina, can you take me a little bit deeper here? Can I ask you this question? Yeah, but what about this part of my life and how does that fit? When I began to see that evolution process or that hunger and desire on the inside of people, it began to be this organic fit. It was not something that was difficult. It was something people were asking for. And I believe that as trainers, as teachers, if you can even just look for where that hunger is at on the inside of the people that you're at, now all of a sudden coaching isn't something that I had to sell. Coaching was something that somebody was asking me for. And so I met the need of where I saw that was going to be a perfect fit. So my first program actually was all centered around purpose identification. Every single person that I've ever met is always asking, why am I here? What's the purpose of my life? How does all of this fit? And so I developed this four-week process of being able to look at someone's personality, their greatest pains, their greatest passions, their most embarrassing moments, all of these different factors, this ingredient mix that I found that if I could put all of that together and make that really personal with someone, their life purpose was literally handed to them on a silver platter. And so it was a four-week process. We went and met once a week. They had homework in between. And after that four-week process, I watched people literally come alive. They knew why they were alive. They knew what their purpose was. And that was a one-on-one program. That's great. Yep. And so I was able to do a really interesting product or service to then move on to coaching. You could create your coaching based on what people are already asking you for advice. I call that your unique brilliance. I mean, we all have our own fingerprints.

## [09:05 - 12:12]

Even your lips have a print to them, your irises. Everything about us is absolutely unique. And so when you begin to watch the people coming to you and saying, Lane, tell me how you do that. That's so easy for you. And it's so hard for me over there. The unique brilliance of you, what comes easy for you, what is natural for you, or what is your area of passion that you love looking into even during your free time, that is usually a huge indicator of what it is that we can end up creating these coaching programs from around. But a lot of my clients will say, that's too easy. It has to be more complicated than that. No, it doesn't. Because our personal areas of passion are something that usually do come easy. Not to say that it's not going to take work because it does. We all know that. But there's an ease. There's a grace. There is an empowerment. There's an anointing, whatever word you want to put with it, that people end up having when they can step into that. And then that is when coaching becomes absolutely fun. I love that. So when you created that first four-week program, how did you decide how much to charge? That pricing is such a touchy subject because I'm sure you know through your personal experience, also through coaching, we don't know how to value ourselves. So how did you go through that process? I did it completely from an analytical position. I love it. I began to look at what does the market say that a life coach should get with this level of experience that I had. And so I ended up starting mine out at just $300 for those four weeks. Now there were four one-hour calls and a lot of work on my side. But I was just beginning. And this was 18 years ago. This is when people were not using life coaching as just a common language in the house. I mean, people used to ask me what I do and I said, I'm a life coach. And they're like, oh, what's for you coach? I mean, it's like, no, I coach you. I get into the inside of you. So it was new back then. So charging $300 at the time to be able to step people into that blew some people away. I mean, I might as well have said $3,000 for some people. You want me to invest $300 on the inside of myself? Well, yeah, if we can identify your life purpose at the end of that four weeks, is $300 worth that? You have to get people out of here and get them into the benefits or what it is that they're going to be receiving. And if you can begin to put a price point on that for the clients that you're serving, that you're loving when you go through that, that's what helped me set that as my price point. And then over the years of coaching, that's just been raised because my area of expertise has increased. I now have coaches who work for me. And so that ladder level, the value ladder that we're taught in order to build, my value increased as my ladder continued to grow. And as you have more people you've worked with successfully and you've seen the transformation and you have those testimonials and you get referrals, then you can start saying, okay, I am worth this because I helped this person do this. If we devalue who we are and if we don't put a monetary value in and connected with that,

## [12:12 - 15:15]

what we're actually telling the people that we're working together with is they're not of value. Giving someone an opportunity to be able to invest into themself, unlock something on the inside of who we are or your money is or your heart will be also, right? We know that. And so when you can begin to invest your own money into your own life, then all of a sudden greatness gets awakened in a completely different way. And so I had to step back and I had to be able to say, you know what, I'm actually robbing and stealing people's openness and breakthrough moment by not allowing them to invest in that themselves. That's a really interesting way to think about it. I really like that because it's turning it on its head. It's also like when we talk about sales and oh, I don't like to sell. Well, you've got something that could change somebody's life and you're not going to tell them about it. That's what sales is. Like telling people the change that you can bring about in their life. So it's really the same way, giving them the opportunity to invest in themselves. What are some of the things you wish you'd known about coaching before you started? If you could go back to the 18 years ago to that, Tina. What would you prep her with? Two different things. I would say number one, coaching has to be first and foremost a realization that when you're coaching somebody else, you're acting as their mirror. You're not their savior. It's not your responsibility. You are there with that person to reflect back to them. You're a co-thinker together with them and you have that opportunity to ask questions, not to give answers, but to pull the answers that are on the inside of them out and by asking the right questions, that's what's going to unlock the treasure on the inside of people. I wish someone would have told me that in my coaching training originally because second thing that I learned the hard way and I'm telling you it was the hard way is I was basing my own personal success or the value of who I was based on the results that my clients were getting. If they were having a great breakthrough moment, yay, I'm a success, but if they weren't breaking through then I was doing something wrong. I had to learn within coaching that me filling my own self up and being able to come into every coaching session with my own identity, with my own solidification on the inside of myself didn't allow for someone else's success or failures to become an identity basis for me. Your breakthrough was for them. I got to just be there in that moment with them. That is something I know a lot of newer coaches struggle with. How can I be a coach if I don't know all the answers? And you said something about you don't have to have the answers, you have to have the questions. Talk more about that because I think that's going to alleviate a lot of fears for people here thinking, well I'm just getting started in this, I don't know all the answers. The nuts and bolts of coaching for me and what I train all of our coaches in is you

## [15:15 - 18:15]

must become a powerful what question asker. So someone can say to you, well I had a really horrible week this week. I'm going to say back to them, what about this week was horrible? A what question acts as a drill to be able to go onto the inside of someone. So if you can become a masterful listener, if you can drill into what it is that person is saying, if you can dial into the emotions that they're expressing in the conversation that they're having with you and literally you're just reflecting back to them and you're giving back to them their own words. Their words are the keys to unlock what's on the inside of them. And just through a powerful what question then all of a sudden you are that mirror back to them giving it back to them and they so often times will go that was a really great question. And I'm like that's exactly what you said. You're helping the person realize it's the treasure in them. What are some of the other skills or traits of a good coach? I'm going to answer that by saying what they're not. That's perfect. Can I do that? Yes, of course. So for me what is not a good coach is going to reveal what is a good coach. What is not a good coach is a person who thinks that it's their job to just give the answer. A good coach is going to help someone find that answer on the inside of themselves. Not a good coach is a person who thinks that it's their job to get in that coaching session and just talk at the person. No, a good coach is going to be one who knows how to be with the person. That person is literally opening up themself and saying, hey, Lane, step into my world for a minute. Come and show me what it is that you're seeing together with me. You're a co-thinker with them. You're not thinking for them. They're asking you questions. You're flipping that back around with a great question. Let me ask that in a different way. You're giving them a perspective shift of being able to see something. Maybe they just need to look at it from another direction. I truly believe that being a coach is about helping every single person realize that every answer that we need is already in us. I've never seen it to be different in 18 years. It's just maybe handing someone a tool. It's not about teaching out. It's not about talking to. It's about being with. It's about listening. It's about reflecting. It's stepping in. If you can do that, and if you truly love people, I believe it's one of the equations that we're missing in the world. There's not a single person that I've ever met that I can't look onto the inside of who they are and be able to see something, a treasure on the inside of them that maybe they haven't seen themself. If I can just hold up that mirror and say, do you see what I see? Can we just hold steady right there? That is coaching for me. I've heard it expressed like holding space. I'll hold the belief for you until you can believe it yourself. I, as the coach, will hold on to this until you grow into it because I see that in you.

## [18:15 - 21:20]

It's a lot like parenting, right? Oh my word. My kids have been some of my greatest teachers within my own life as well. Absolutely. I would think that a lot of people who are listening to this and say, oh my gosh, I would love that. Maybe what I've heard termed impacts where they pick up on their highly emotional intelligence, but they have trouble drawing that boundary to protect themselves. So if the client's having a bad day, the client's sad, the client's going through something, they pick up on that and it weighs on them. How have you been able to put boundaries around yourself so you're not carrying home the weight of your client? When I first started out, I could only coach three people in a day. It would maxed me out. I was trying to figure out, I mean it was this constant rhythm of balance of trying to understand until I realized that, have you ever heard of that cup and saucer analogy that you have to make sure that you yourself as the cup, like right here, you are so full that you begin to pour over. I haven't heard that before described like that. So if you as the coach are the full cup and everything that you allow to pour over and be caught within the beautiful saucer underneath that cup, if you give your clients the opportunity to be able to come and drink from your saucer, but never from your cup, you are going to completely have a different experience. As soon as I learned that principle of coming full to every single call that I had or every single meeting that I had and allow someone to just sit in that space of my saucer together with me and we stepped into that space together with them, now all of a sudden the balance was there. It wasn't exhausting. It was completely filling. However, if I ever came to a call and my cup was not all the way full, what I found were that my clients was like that dried sponge from the kitchen. They would jump into my cup together with me because they were so thirsty and hungry, that I didn't have anything to give to them on the outside, then that's what dried me up the most. What does your business look like now? So when you started coaching, it sounds like you were teaching courses. Coaching was a natural evolution of that. What does the business look like now? I went through a major evolution process within our coaching company due to the pandemic that the whole entire world went through. Our coaching used to be done or our classes used to be done all in person. And just like everybody else in the world, when all of a sudden we could no longer meet in person and hotel conference rooms were shut down here within the United States, literally my whole entire books of classes that had been scheduled for the next year was gone. And so I went into this huge seeking mode of trying to figure out what am I going to do? I hadn't done online classes before. I'd never done anything. Everything had been in person. I'm just such a touchy-feely person. And that's when I got introduced to Tony and Dean. So I began to go through the process of learning how I could bring this knowledge business

that I had, but this coaching practice that I hadn't. Will this really work if I can do it online? Will this really work if I can do this over Zoom? Would it really work? And bringing our course then into mastermind. And then that has evolved now into an entire business. And so we have one-on-one coaching, soul detox. We have multiple masterminds, five different masterminds that we are running, all virtual, all via Zoom. Because what I realized is that we had the center rungs of our ladder. Our Soul Detox program was $3,000 in order to jump in. And for some people that was too big of a jump.
